name: La Felguera, Alto de la Mozqueta, and Mieres Loop
name_original: La Felguera, Alto de la Mozqueta y Mieres – circular desde Langreo
description: Cycle the challenging La Felguera, Alto de la Mozqueta y Mieres route from Langreo, a 29.2-mile ride through Asturias' scenic hills.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Llangreu/Langreo traverse diverse landscapes, including scenic Asturian hills and the expansive Nalón Valley. The region features challenging climbs and rewarding descents through extensive native forests and limestone formations. Many routes integrate elements of the area's industrial heritage, providing unique backdrops for cycling. The terrain offers varied experiences for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Llangreu/Langreo The most popular road cycling route is La Felguera, Alto de la Mozqueta, and Mieres Loop, a 29.2 miles (47.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 39 minutes to complete. This difficult route features a significant elevation gain and offers views of the Nalón River and Valley. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Up, up, up it goes – Alto del Padrún loop from La Felguera, a moderate 30.6 miles (49.2 km) path. This route presents a challenging climb through the Asturian hills. Local road cyclists also love the Soto de Agues Village – Tanes Village and Church loop from La Felguera - Nuevu Llangréu, a 44.0 miles (70.9 km) trail leading through the Nalón Valley, often completed in about 3 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Llangreu/Langreo is defined by Asturian hills, the Nalón Valley, and wooded areas. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels. The routes in Llangreu/Langreo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 900 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Llangreu/Langreo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Llangreu/Langreo?
A: Llangreu/Langreo offers a diverse network of over 30 road cycling routes. These routes cater to various skill levels, with 3 easy, 17 moderate, and 11 difficult options.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Llangreu/Langreo?
A: Road cycling in Llangreu/Langreo primarily involves traversing scenic Asturian hills and the expansive Nalón Valley. You'll encounter a mix of challenging climbs and rewarding descents, often passing through extensive native forests and wooded areas. The routes are generally well-paved, making them suitable for road bikes.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes in the region?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1078368225/la-felguera-alto-de-la-mozqueta-and-mieres-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Felguera, Alto de la Mozqueta, and Mieres Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gains over 47 km. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38183637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de La Faya de Los Lobos – Quintana loop from Sama&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 74 km with substantial climbing.

Q: Are there any moderate road cycling routes suitable for intermediate riders?
A: Yes, there are several moderate routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14998044" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Up, up, up it goes – Alto del Padrún loop from La Felguera&lt;/a&gt; is a popular choice, covering 49.2 km with a challenging climb through the Asturian hills. Another great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40325117" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soto de Agues Village – Tanes Village and Church loop from La Felguera - Nuevu Llangréu&lt;/a&gt;, a 70.9 km route through the Nalón Valley.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I see along the cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer expansive views of the Nalón Valley and Peña del Aramo, especially from elevated points like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1074608" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Urbiés Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. You'll also cycle through very wooded and solitary places, characteristic of the Asturian hills and valleys. The region is known for its well-preserved trails navigating lively rural landscapes and mountain passes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Llangreu/Langreo?
A: The best time to road cycle in Llangreu/Langreo is generally from spring through autumn. This period avoids the hottest summer days and the potentially icy winter conditions that can occur on the significant climbs. The weather during these seasons is typically more favorable for outdoor activities.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Llangreu/Langreo?
A: Yes, many routes in the region are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1078368225/la-felguera-alto-de-la-mozqueta-and-mieres-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Felguera, Alto de la Mozqueta, and Mieres Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40381148" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de La Colladiella – Pozo Fortuna Memorial loop from Ciañu&lt;/a&gt;. These circular routes provide a convenient way to explore the area.

Q: Can I combine road cycling with exploring the region's industrial heritage?
A: Absolutely. Llangreu/Langreo subtly integrates its industrial past into the landscape. Some cycling routes, like the 'Old Railway Path' (which intersects with other paths), explicitly explore this heritage. You'll find unique backdrops that blend natural beauty with elements of industrial history, such as facilities like the Iron and Steel Museum or the Ecomuseum of the Samuño Valley.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Llangreu/Langreo?
A: The road cycling routes in Llangreu/Langreo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, challenging climbs, and the unique integration of natural beauty with industrial heritage.

Q: Are there options for parking or public transport access to the routes?
A: While specific parking details for each route vary, many routes start from towns like La Felguera, Sama, or Ciañu, which typically offer parking facilities. The region's infrastructure also includes connecting paths that intersect with other cycling routes, suggesting potential access points via public transport, though specific details would depend on your starting location.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes for beginners in Llangreu/Langreo?
A: Yes, Llangreu/Langreo offers 3 easy road cycling routes, perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes provide a gentler introduction to the region's beautiful landscapes without the significant elevation gains found on more challenging trails.
tips: Beautiful views of the Nalón river as we pass through the city of Langreo at the height of the Miramar bridge.
At this point the road runs like a balcony over the valley and Urbiés and all the surrounding farmhouses can be seen in the background. From this slope you can see the whole green pot below.

faqs.question: How challenging is this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route is classified as difficult for road cycling, requiring very good fitness. It covers approximately 47 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of about 1,080 meters, primarily due to the demanding Alto de la Mozqueta climb.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this route?
faqs.answer: On average, cyclists can expect to complete this route in about 2 hours and 39 minutes. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The starting point of the route in Langreo is conveniently located next to a parking lot, making it easy to access and begin your ride.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience very scenic views throughout the Asturian hills. The route transitions from old industrial valleys to areas of fine natural scenery and vibrant green countryside. As you climb Alto de la Mozqueta, you'll find fantastic, very wooded, solitary places, and from the summit, expansive views of the Nalón Valley and Peña del Aramo.

faqs.question: Are there any specific landmarks or points of interest on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you'll pass by some notable spots. Keep an eye out for the View of the Nalón River from Puente del Miramar and the Urbiés Viewpoint, both offering great photo opportunities.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While specific seasonal recommendations aren't provided, Asturias generally offers good cycling conditions from spring through autumn. Given the significant climbs, avoiding the hottest summer days or potentially icy winter conditions would be advisable for the most enjoyable experience.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for road cycling, and while there are no explicit prohibitions against dogs, it's generally not suitable for cycling with pets due to the demanding nature of the ride and traffic considerations on public roads. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular adventure.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this area?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. While the area is part of the Cuencas Mineras Protected Landscape, it's generally open for public access without specific charges for recreational activities like cycling.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect with several other paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the Vía Verde del Valle de Turón, Vuelta a Langreo I, Circular de Ciaño, and the Paséu fluvial del Nalón, among others.

faqs.question: What is the most challenging section of the Alto de la Mozqueta climb?
faqs.answer: The most challenging section of the Alto de la Mozqueta climb is when approached from Ciaño. The last 6 kilometers average a consistent 8.5% gradient, with frequent sections reaching double-digit percentages. The segment around La Fresnosa is particularly spectacular, featuring several hairpin bends with steep gradients.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is not suitable for beginner cyclists. It is classified as 'difficult' with significant elevation gain and demanding climbs, particularly the Alto de la Mozqueta, which requires very good fitness and experience on challenging road terrain.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments or supplies along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through towns like La Felguera, Mieres, and Langreo, where you will find various amenities including cafes, shops, and places to resupply. It's always a good idea to carry enough water and snacks, especially for the more remote climbing sections.

waypoints.name: View of the Nalón River from Puente del Miramar
